ud2.10:1.2: on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Anupiyā in the Mango Grove.
ud2.10:1.3:
And on that occasion, Ven. Bhaddiya, Kāḷigodhā’s son, on going to the wilderness,
to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, would repeatedly exclaim,
ud2.10:2.1:
A large number of monks heard Ven. Bhaddiya, Kāḷigodhā’s son, on going to the wilderness,
to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, repeatedly exclaim,
ud2.10:2.2:
“What bliss!
What bliss!” and on hearing him, the thought occurred to them,
ud2.10:2.4: “There’s no doubt but that Ven. Bhaddiya, Kāḷigodhā’s son, doesn’t enjoy leading the holy life,
ud2.10:3.1:
So they went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side.
As they were sitting there, they told him,
ud2.10:3.2:
“Ven.
Bhaddiya, Kāḷigodhā’s son, lord, on going to the wilderness,
to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, repeatedly exclaims,
ud2.10:3.4: There’s no doubt but that Ven. Bhaddiya doesn’t enjoy leading the holy life,
ud2.10:4.1: Then the Blessed One told a certain monk,
ud2.10:4.2:
“Come, monk.
In my name, call Bhaddiya, saying,
ud2.10:5.1:
Responding,
“As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, the monk went to Ven. Bhaddiya, Kāḷigodhā’s son, and on arrival he said to him,
ud2.10:5.3:
Responding,
“As you say, my friend,” to the monk, Ven. Bhaddiya, Kāḷigodhā’s son,
went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side.
As he was sitting there, the Blessed One said to him,
ud2.10:6.1:
“Is it true, Bhaddiya that–on going to the wilderness,
to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling–you repeatedly exclaim,
ud2.10:7.1:
“What compelling reason do you have in mind that–when going to the wilderness,
to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling–you repeatedly exclaim,
ud2.10:7.3:
“Before, when I has a householder, maintaining the bliss of kingship, lord,
I had guards posted within and without the royal apartments,
within and without the city, within and without the countryside.
ud2.10:7.4: But even though I was thus guarded, thus protected, I dwelled in fear–agitated, distrustful, & afraid.
ud2.10:7.5:
But now, on going alone to the wilderness, to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, I dwell without fear,
unagitated, confident, & unafraid–unconcerned, unruffled, living on the gifts of others, with my mind like a wild deer.
ud2.10:7.6:
This is the compelling reason I have in mind that–when going to the wilderness,
to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling–I repeatedly exclaim,
ud2.10:8.1: Then, on realizing the significance of that, the Blessed One on that occasion exclaimed:
ud2.10:9.1: From whose heart there is no provocation,
ud2.10:9.2: & for whom becoming & non-becoming are overcome,
ud2.10:9.3: he– beyond fear, blissful, with no grief–
ud2.10:9.4: is one the devas can’t see.
